title: Shinjis Actions
wikitext:
[]: Shinji's Actions             Quote Post by Kendrix » Tue Feb 02, 2021 8:51 pm BusterMachine4 wrote: And besides all that, it would also be a complete contradiction of the rules of storytelling. If you give a character a crushing revelation, one that weighs down on their psyche and motivates their actions, you can't just have that revelation revealed to be false without it feeling like a cop-out. It's like if in Return of the Jedi, Luke asked Yoda, "Is Darth Vader my father?" and Yoda responded, "Nah, he was lying to you. Vader actually did kill your father. Go get revenge!" That version of the movie wouldn't be anywhere near as good as the version we got.

[]: Re: How Can Shinji Help Wille Without Piloting An Eva?             Quote Post by Settie » Tue Feb 02, 2021 9:23 pm BusterMachine4 wrote: And besides all that, it would also be a complete contradiction of the rules of storytelling. If you give a character a crushing revelation, one that weighs down on their psyche and motivates their actions, you can't just have that revelation revealed to be false without it feeling like a cop-out.

[]: Re: How Can Shinji Help Wille Without Piloting An Eva?             Quote Post by BusterMachine4 » Tue Feb 02, 2021 9:50 pm Settie wrote: Yeah it's almost like the intentions of those making the revelations have everything to do with it and it isn't a cop-out to point that out. Kaworu needed Shinji to pilot so he gave him information to lead him there. Fuyutsuki did the same thought for different reasons. In order for Shinji to be responsible for the world of 3.0, you need to ignore the end of 2.0 and all the shenanigans involving the eldritch abomination that is the fused Mark6-12th angel-lilith.

[]: Re: How Can Shinji Help Wille Without Piloting An Eva?             Quote Post by BernardoCairo » Tue Feb 02, 2021 10:05 pm Kendrix wrote: plus he's still acting like a child at times, mainly because he is at the tail end of the age range where childish behavior is normal. It's frustrating - you're in a burning house and your annoying younger sibling wants to get their toys and you'll have to follow them if they run off into the fire... Except they started the fire (even if it was because Papa let him play with the matches) and if they run off there will be more fire

[]: Re: How Can Shinji Help Wille Without Piloting An Eva?             Quote Post by Settie » Tue Feb 02, 2021 10:16 pm Kaworu not lying is a mere technicality. At best he mislead Shinji in order to make him happy. In actuality he gaslit him with cherry picked information and insinuations. He straight up told him he was the catalyst of what he saw, is that technically true in round about way? Yes, but that's a farm cry from being solely responsible as Kaworu insinuated. It's not going back on something if it wasn't entirely true in the first place. The SW example would be more comparable if Vader tuned out to be his adoptive father after killing his biological one. Technically true but not the whole story. Anyway this is getting offtopic.

[]: Re: How Can Shinji Help Wille Without Piloting An Eva?             Quote Post by BusterMachine4 » Tue Feb 02, 2021 10:38 pm Settie wrote: Kaworu not lying is a mere technicality. At best he mislead Shinji in order to make him happy. In actuality he gaslit him with cherry picked information and insinuations. He straight up told him he was the catalyst of what he saw, is that technically true in round about way? Yes, but that's a farm cry from being solely responsible as Kaworu insinuated.

[]: Re: How Can Shinji Help Wille Without Piloting An Eva?             Quote Post by FXArmaros98 » Wed Feb 03, 2021 4:40 am There were a bunch of emotions in the air (especially in the case of Misato and Asuka). But that doesn't mean that everyone just hates him or doesn't care about his existence.

[]: Re: How Can Shinji Help Wille Without Piloting An Eva?             Quote Post by Kendrix » Wed Feb 03, 2021 6:24 pm I still think any variation of "kaworu is lying/manipulating" is super contrived and doesn't even pass the occam's razor test especially if all the emotional torque of Q rests on those words being true. If Shinji could have done anything different, it would have had to be by getting Asuka out of Bardiel or not running off afterwards. Then maybe a three on one attack by Shinji, Rei and Mari or Asuka could have taken Zeruel down. By the time he got into the EVA it was probably too late already - if he hadn't awakened the EVA, could he have beaten Zeruel any other way? We don't know if "things going like the original series" was really an option compatible with rebuild specific lore, especially if eva 01 turns out to be one of the adams after all. It's also unclear what Yui's involvement was. Did she want this? Could she even have stopped it? Or is it somewhere in between like, she didn't foresee all consequences, or she had to choose between absorbing Shinji and letting him die of his wounds (we all saw that fountain of blood in the silhouette shot of the entry plug when Zeruel skewered EVA 01, didn't we? He was prolly 5 mins from dying of internal bleeding throughout the whole thing.)

[]: Re: How Can Shinji Help Wille Without Piloting An Eva?             Quote Post by ElMariachi » Wed Feb 03, 2021 7:36 pm Kendrix wrote: I still think any variation of "kaworu is lying/manipulating" is super contrived and doesn't even pass the occam's razor test especially if all the emotional torque of Q rests on those words being true.

[]: Re: How Can Shinji Help Wille Without Piloting An Eva?             Quote Post by BusterMachine4 » Wed Feb 03, 2021 7:52 pm ElMariachi wrote: Except that Kaworu's implications when he showed to Shinji what happened to Tokyo-3 is in contradiction with everything we see later, and even Kaworu's own words once they reach Central Dogma!

[]: Re: How Can Shinji Help Wille Without Piloting An Eva?             Quote Post by ShinjiStranglesAsuka » Wed Feb 03, 2021 7:55 pm Shinji: I didn't intend for any of this. Qaworu: That's not what the Lilin think. I'm paraphrasing a bit obviously but this is the most important bit of conversation between the two as it pertains to this topic. We'll see whether or not people really hold Shinji responsible.

[]: Re: How Can Shinji Help Wille Without Piloting An Eva?             Quote Post by EscapismIsBad » Wed Feb 03, 2021 8:20 pm By the time he got into the EVA it was probably too late already - if he hadn't awakened the EVA, could 'he have beaten Zeruel any other way?' We don't know if "things going like the original series" was really an option compatible with rebuild specific lore, especially if eva 01 turns out to be one of the adams after all

[]: Re: How Can Shinji Help Wille Without Piloting An Eva?             Quote Post by Settie » Wed Feb 03, 2021 9:28 pm ^ Thing is that a pseudo-awakened Unit1 had already rekt the 10th, it was only Shinjis desperation for a W that started an impact by forcefully trying to get Rei back. If he had stopped and simply destroyed the core, there would be a pseudo-awakened eva but no impact.

[]: Re: How Can Shinji Help Wille Without Piloting An Eva?             Quote Post by Kendrix » Wed Feb 03, 2021 10:24 pm First, that's such a dismissive and cruel way to talk about someone wanting to help their comrade, reeks of hindsight bias and strikes me something no reasonable person would come up with if they first saw the movie if they weren't already invested in particular interpretations that never made sense even for the original show, this overfocus on EVA as a "fuck you" to people. Also, it was that desire that let him awaken the EVA in the first place. I don't think calmly sitting there & intellectually telling himself that he ought to beat the angel because that's what Jesus would do would've done the trick, it required some emotional catalyst He simply couldn't control it (it's almost as if the whole point... and central motif... of the EVAs is that they're barely controllable or understandeable); especially since he was never told that there was a need to stop or else. He didn't even know what actually happened until Kaworu told him, like oh, finally, one person explains. The main reason I really really HATE this interpretation though it's that it basically comes down to thought crime. As well as possibly a western-centric misunderstanding. Because it's the same unforgiving, utterly misanthropic stance that I hate about certain organized religions or any other sort of purity culture & drives the neuroses & self esteem problems of people everywhere, that if your intentions aren't 100% spotless and selfless they don't count and they're doomed from the start. It's like when ppl descry Sayaka from Madoka as "selfish" it's the very fact that Sayaka held herself to impossible standards in the first place that drove her mad. And Shinji wasn't told any more about what an EVA really is than the girls heard about what becoming a magical girl actually entails. Because this wasn't an explicit choice like the end of "life is strange". The "I don't care what happens.." line is more about his motivation. It doesn't matter if your motivation is to make money or because you love the job as long as you do it well. If you cut corners out of greed that's bad but the mere fact that you don't love your menial desk job and care only about your hobbies & family doesn't make you irresponsible. Like, I very highly doubt that what Anno & co meant to tell us by showing how *every single character* has some personal, emotional motivation underneath their socially acceptable guises of ambition, professionalism or selflessness was that you should be perfectly selfless. I'd even say that you completely missed the point if you think that. The statement that's being made here is that most humans are like this. That this is human, to need something to keep going. That we should maybe be self-aware about it and seek it, instead of waiting for it to happen while doing indirect acts. ("waiting for someone to "give" you happiness, as its put in ep 25) Like you think it's a coincidence how Shinji's wording in that scene calls back to that last conversation with Misato? Or that she cheers him on? Having his priorities figured out, being honest about it is something laudable. He can only make his decision based on what he known, and the reason it ends up having horrid consequences is something he didn't know. Also he didn't start piloting mostly so he'd get bonds at that moment.He only admitted it. It was always the case. At most he shifted from pleasing Gendo who doesn't respond to someone who does. The closest one to idealism/altruism is Kaworu and he's basically there for Shinji to envy him. Plus he still has his own desire of freedom. And in the end the exact opposite happened. He expected to die but at least the last thing he'd do is save Rei. But the effect was that he survived and everyone else died, Rei included. If you label a nuke button "switch train track so it doesn't flatten your friend who protected you from a giant laser earlier", you can't judge the person as if they had deliberately made the choice to fire nukes, no matter what they were thinking when they pressed it. We don't know if "just destroying the core" would have worked or if the EVA was just bound to keep and keep transforming. We don't know if he could've stopped after pulling Rei out if he'd known the awakening was dangerous. We don't even know if what happened didn't depend on factors between NTI and actual TI that Shinji had no way to influence. If no one had plopped the cassius out of EVA 01, it might just have remained sealed forever.

[]: Re: How Can Shinji Help Wille Without Piloting An Eva?             Quote Post by BusterMachine4 » Thu Feb 04, 2021 1:09 am Kendrix, you do have some very unconventional views. Don't agree with them, though. I don't think your idea that the series is supposed to have no moral message whatsoever really holds up to scrutiny. Of course, the hard-core "WILLE was right, Shinji was wrong" people are ignoring a huge chunk of the movie. But it seems like you're also ignoring huge chunks of the franchise in order to support your view of it. Shinji's mental state is supposed to be wrong. In fact, everyone's mental state is supposed to be wrong, but Shinji is the main character so the focus is on him. He's been shown throughout the franchise to have a need for connection, a need that drives all the pain in his life. He pilots the Eva because he wants to get affection for free instead of earning it. Even as the Eva causes pain to everyone around him, he still pilots it, because at least that pain helps him feel something . And that attitude still hasn't changed in Rebuild. Despite you defending Shinji's actions in the "saving Rei" scene as being pure and good, that's not really what the movie itself portrays. You said that no one told him to stop, but Ritsuko actually did tell him to stop. However, Shinji was so determined to save Rei, the one person who showed the most connection to him, that he inadvertently sacrificed the world. He selfishly threw away everything, just to feel connection again. It's the same problem that Shinji has had the entire franchise. Shinji's need is made abundantly clear by the dialogue: he needs to get out of his selfish mindset, learn more about the way things work. Shinji in the original series was able to learn this lesson, now the only question is whether Rebuild Shinji will be able to learn it as well. And I also resent the implication that Eva having a moral somehow means it's "finger-wagging," or a "fuck you." Like I said, everyone's mental state is supposed to be wrong. Nowhere is this better demonstrated than 3.0: every character had some role to play in Fourth Impact starting. Eva can have important lessons about getting out of unhealthy mental states without it being a "middle finger to otaku" or whatever stupid moral the Reddit crowd came up with. You say this interpretation is "cruel," but I'd say the idea of all this darkness happening for no controllable reason is even more cruel. If there's no moral to be learned, then why did Third Impact happen, just to play a cruel joke on Shinji? I'd much prefer for the characters to be in charge of their own destiny.

[]: Re: How Can Shinji Help Wille Without Piloting An Eva?             Quote Post by ShinjiStranglesAsuka » Thu Feb 04, 2021 2:09 am Nice BM4. Although I will say that from reading around the old posts on these forums and discord, people really seem to have had it out for rebuild Shinji. The only thing I can truly fault him for is pulling the spears. His handling of the 9th and 10th Angels were a bit dubious but not unquestionably so. I've seen arguments that he should have killed Asuka along with the 9th and let Rei die along with the 10th. While they're technically the right actions, they're also fucking whack - especially from the perspective of a little kid who've just recently began to bond with other people.

[]: Re: How Can Shinji Help Wille Without Piloting An Eva?             Quote Post by BusterMachine4 » Thu Feb 04, 2021 3:12 am ShinjiStranglesAsuka wrote: Although I will say that from reading around the old posts on these forums and discord, people really seem to have had it out for rebuild Shinji. The only thing I can truly fault him for is pulling the spears. His handling of the 9th and 10th Angels were a bit dubious but not unquestionably so. I've seen arguments that he should have killed Asuka along with the 9th and let Rei die along with the 10th. While they're technically the right actions, they're also fucking whack - especially from the perspective of a little kid who've just recently began to bond with other people.

[]: Re: How Can Shinji Help Wille Without Piloting An Eva?             Quote Post by Kendrix » Thu Feb 04, 2021 4:04 am BusterMachine4 wrote: no moral message whatsoever

[]: Re: How Can Shinji Help Wille Without Piloting An Eva?             Quote Post by BusterMachine4 » Thu Feb 04, 2021 7:53 am You say that’s the message, but there’s been very little to support it so far. If Third Impact was supposed to be some unchangeable event, completely beyond anyone’s control, why was it necessary for Shinji to be the catalyst? They could have just had Nerv initiate the project during the time skip if that’s what they were going for. But instead, they had our main protagonist initiate it. I have to imagine that means something. And I still don’t like you smearing the idea of a moral about self-improvement and avoiding destructive mindsets with phrases like “2D” or “get-off-my-lawn style.” I already acknowledged that Eva is much more complicated than simple good vs evil. It’s just that the self-improvement moral seems so obvious and in your face to me that I can’t understand how you think the fans just made it up.

[]: Re: How Can Shinji Help Wille Without Piloting An Eva?             Quote Post by BernardoCairo » Thu Feb 04, 2021 7:53 am BusterMachine4 wrote: That's also true. The "Shinji is a perfect baby" people are obviously wrong, but the "Shinji is a horrible brat" people are just as wrong.

However, Shinji was so determined to save Rei, the one person who showed the most connection to him, that he inadvertently sacrificed the world. He selfishly threw away everything, just to feel connection again. It's the same problem that Shinji has had the entire franchise. Shinji's need is made abundantly clear by the dialogue: he needs to get out of his selfish mindset, learn more about the way things work. Shinji in the original series was able to learn this lesson, now the only question is whether Rebuild Shinji will be able to learn it as well. And I also resent the implication that Eva having a moral somehow means it's "finger-wagging," or a "fuck you." Like I said, everyone's mental state is supposed to be wrong. Nowhere is this better demonstrated than 3.0: every character had some role to play in Fourth Impact starting. Eva can have important lessons about getting out of unhealthy mental states without it

Article: Shinjis Actions
 said, everyone's mental state is supposed to be wrong. Nowhere is this better demonstrated than 3.0: every character had some role to play in Fourth Impact starting. Eva can have important lessons about getting out of unhealthy mental states without it being a "middle finger to otaku" or whatever stupid moral the Reddit crowd came up with. You say this interpretation is "cruel," but I'd say the idea of all this darkness happening for no controllable reason is even more cruel. If there's no moral to be learned, then why did Third Impact happen, just to play a cruel joke on Shinji? I'd much prefer for the characters to be in charge of their own destiny.
